content.text: CrewAI Flows (introduced in 0.63) is a structured event-driven orchestration layer that sits above individual crews. Where a
manages a single pipeline of agents and tasks, a
manages multiple pipelines, conditional branches, and state — connecting crews and plain Python steps into a coherent application.

content.text: A Flow is a Python class. Each method decorated with
object (a Pydantic model) and are wired together by decorators instead of explicit function calls.

from crewai.flow.flow import Flow, listen, start, router, or_
from pydantic import BaseModel
Container for all steps and shared state
Typed shared data accessible to every step
First step(s) to execute when flow.kickoff() is called
Step that runs after another step completes
Step that returns a string to select the next branch
Step that runs when any of the listed steps completes
Step that runs only when ALL listed steps complete
return value of its trigger step
as its first positional argument. The shared
is always accessible regardless of which step is running.
Embedding a Crew Inside a Flow Step
The most common Flow pattern is to use plain Python steps for data preparation and routing, and
calls for the heavy AI work.

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew
from crewai.flow.flow import Flow, listen, start
from pydantic import BaseModel

class PipelineState(BaseModel):
    topic: str = ""
    research: str = ""
    report: str = ""

class PipelineFlow(Flow[PipelineState]):

    @start()
    def receive_topic(self):
        self.state.topic = "renewable energy investment trends"

    @listen(receive_topic)
    def run_research_crew(self):
        researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", goal="Research {topic}", backstory="...")
        task = Task(
            description="Research {topic} thoroughly",
            expected_output="Comprehensive notes",
            agent=researcher,
        )
        crew = Crew(agents=[researcher], tasks=[task])
        result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": self.state.topic})
        self.state.research = result.raw

    @listen(run_research_crew)
    def run_writing_crew(self):
        writer = Agent(role="Writer", goal="Write a report", backstory="...")
        task = Task(
            description="Write a report using: {research}",
            expected_output="1000-word report",
            agent=writer,
        )
        crew = Crew(agents=[writer], tasks=[task])
        result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"research": self.state.research})
        self.state.report = result.raw
        return self.state.report

flow = PipelineFlow()
report = flow.kickoff()
print(report)
A router step inspects the current state and returns a string — the name of the next branch to execute. Steps listen to specific return values using the
decorator, filtered by the router's return string.
to trigger a step as soon as
listed steps have completed — useful for joining parallel branches.
Flows vs. Crews — When to Use Each
Typed Pydantic model shared across all steps
to run the whole flow asynchronously.

import asyncio
from crewai.flow.flow import Flow, listen, start

class AsyncFlow(Flow):

    @start()
    async def step_one(self):
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # simulate I/O
        return "step one done"

    @listen(step_one)
    async def step_two(self, result: str):
        return f"step two received: {result}"

async def main():
    flow = AsyncFlow()
    output = await flow.kickoff_async()
    print(output)

asyncio.run(main())
CrewAI can render a flow's DAG as an HTML diagram. Call

flow = ResearchFlow()
flow.plot("research_flow")   # writes research_flow.html
# Open in a browser to see an interactive node graph of all steps and edges
The flow's state type is declared as a generic parameter:
in any step. You can pre-populate the state by passing it at kickoff.
Flows support state persistence so they can resume after a failure. Use

import uuid

flow_id = str(uuid.uuid4())

flow = ResearchFlow()
# On first run, state is saved to disk with flow_id as the key
result = flow.kickoff(flow_id=flow_id)

# If the run fails mid-way, resume from the last checkpoint:
flow_resumed = ResearchFlow()
result = flow_resumed.kickoff(flow_id=flow_id)  # skips completed steps
by default. This is useful for long-running flows (minutes to hours) where transient failures would otherwise require a full restart.
methods. All of them are triggered simultaneously when
is called — they run in parallel.
Steps are plain Python methods, so standard try/except works. Catch exceptions inside a step and update the state to signal failure to downstream steps.
Combining Multiple Crews in One Flow
A Flow shines when an application needs two or more distinct AI pipelines to collaborate. Each pipeline is a separate Crew; the Flow controls when each runs and shares state between them.
for setup and teardown logic that runs before the first step and after the last step.
Unit-test individual steps by calling them directly on a flow instance with a pre-set state. No

import pytest
from crewai.flow.flow import Flow, listen, start

def test_write_intro_appends_section():
    flow = DocumentFlow()
    flow.state.title = "Test Document"

    # Call the step directly
    flow.write_intro()

    assert len(flow.state.sections) == 1
    assert "Test Document" in flow.state.sections[0]
    assert flow.state.word_count > 0

def test_router_returns_pass_for_long_draft():
    flow = QualityFlow()
    flow.state.draft = " ".join(["word"] * 60)  # 60-word draft

    result = flow.check_quality()

    assert result == "pass"
    assert flow.state.quality == "pass"

enables conditional branching by returning a string that selects the next listener branch.

Use a Flow when you need branching, parallel joins, or multi-pipeline state; use a plain Crew for linear multi-agent pipelines.
